question: What is the primary evaluation function used in Greedy Best-First Search?
options: f(n) = g(n) (cost from start to node)
f(n) = h(n) (heuristic cost to goal)
f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
f(n) = estimated total cost
explanation: Greedy Best-First Search is 'greedy' because it selects the node that appears to be closest to the goal, using only the heuristic function f(n) = h(n) to make its decision, ignoring the cost incurred to reach that node.

question: Which of the following is a key disadvantage of Greedy Best-First Search?
options: It has a high time complexity.
It is not complete and can get stuck in infinite loops.
It does not use a heuristic function.
It always finds the optimal path.
explanation: Unlike algorithms like A*, Greedy Best-First Search is not complete. It can be misled by an inaccurate heuristic and may become stuck following an infinite path that never reaches the goal, or it may find a path that is not the cheapest.

question: In which scenario is Greedy Best-First Search most likely to perform well and find a solution quickly?
options: When the heuristic function h(n) is always 0.
When the heuristic function is admissible and consistent.
When the search space is a perfectly balanced binary tree.
When the goal is to minimize the total cost g(n) of the path.
explanation: The algorithm performs well when a good heuristic (one that provides a reliable estimate of the distance to the goal) is available. An admissible and consistent heuristic helps guide the search efficiently towards the goal, though it still doesn't guarantee optimality.

question: How does the memory usage (space complexity) of Greedy Best-First Search typically compare to Breadth-First Search (BFS) in the worst case?
options: It uses significantly less memory than BFS.
It uses about the same amount of memory as BFS.
It uses more memory than BFS because it stores heuristic values.
Its memory usage is unrelated to the branching factor.
explanation: In the worst case, both algorithms have an O(b^m) space complexity, where b is the branching factor and m is the maximum depth of the search tree. Both may need to store all generated nodes in the worst-case scenario before finding a solution.

question: Consider a heuristic function where h(n) is the Euclidean distance to the goal. If Greedy Best-First Search expands a node N, which has a neighbor M that is closer to the goal in a straight line but requires a high-cost detour to reach, what is the likely behavior of the algorithm?
options: It will ignore M because its g(n) cost is too high.
It will prioritize expanding M next because its h(n) value is lower.
It will calculate f(n)=g(n)+h(n) for M before deciding.
It will mark N as visited and never reconsider it.
explanation: This question highlights the core limitation of the algorithm. Since it only considers the heuristic h(n), it will be immediately attracted to node M, which has a smaller straight-line distance to the goal. It ignores the fact that the actual path cost to M (and beyond) might be much higher, potentially leading it away from the truly optimal path.
